issue_title: Format description and payload data with newlines
[Documentation Issue]
issue_body:
Page Name: storage-acl
Release: 0.8.0

Please add an explanation and code snippet to illustrate how to set the ACL for an object. The referenced page only talks about setting bucket-level permissions, and it isn't easy to find whether a blob object also has the acl property, and whether it works the same way as it does with buckets. I would suggest a code snipped such as the one below, which uploads a new blob and sets its ACL to "public read". It's also important to note that the blob needs to be uploaded before its ACL can be set (i.e. if I move the "upload_from_string()" command below acl.save(), the latter will not have any effect, nor will it fail with any errors).

def put_object(bucket_name, object_name, object, public_read = True):
	client = storage.Client(PROJECT)
	bucket = client.get_bucket(bucket_name)
	blob = bucket.blob(object_name)
	blob.upload_from_string(object,content_type = 'image/jpeg', client = client)
	# set ACL to public-read so the image can be accessed by any user
	if (public_read):
		acl = blob.acl
		acl.all().grant_read()
		acl.save()

issue_title: Fix Storage naming to (mostly) match API Schema
issue_body:
- [x] Renaming noun "key" to "object" or close relative (#560).
- [x] Remove `boto` aliases (#568)
- [ ] Address other concerning naming conventions

(Above added by @dhermes on 1/19/2014)

----

The naming conventions appear to be modeled around s3's boto library, but the library is not compatible with S3. It seems like it might be more useful to use the JSON API resource names. 
The major difference here is the use of "key" to represent an "object" resource.  The Cloud Storage API JSON docs typically use the word "key" to refer to an API key or a service account private key file, never to a cloud object resource.  Of course "object" is a reserved name in Python, but there are other possibilities.
With the hierarchy of connection/bucket/key, this looks a lot like boto without actually supporting s3, which could confuse users.

/cc @thobrla

While using the python google-cloud-bigtable to read a large collection of data (10M to 90M rows of at least 4MB to 5MB per row), I noticed a strange behavior: The used RAM was at lease twice the size of the data I was reading from the table.

```python

2. OS type and version: GKE / COS
3. Python version and virtual environment information: 3.5 running on Kubernetes Engine
4. google-cloud-python version: google-cloud-pubsub==0.30.1
6. Steps to reproduce: When running workers in GKE to process items (cloud storage blob paths) from PubSub and scaling them (by scaling a Kubernetes Job), new workers don't pick any work. To have workers distribute the work among them they have to be present when the work gets produced. Later joining workers just sit and idle.
7. Code example


```python
def receive_messages():
    def callback(message):
        logging.info("Message {} acknowledged".format(message.data.decode()))
        # It's fine for me to ack fast, as the state of the import gets written

        message.ack()
        do_hard_work(message.data.decode())
        global NUM_ITEMS
        if NUM_ITEMS > 25:
            collected = gc.collect()
            logging.info("Garbage collector: collected {:d} objects.".format(collected))

    flow_control = pubsub_v1.types.FlowControl(max_messages=2)
    executor = futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)
    policy = pubsub_v1.subscriber.policy.thread.Policy(SUBSCRIBER, 
                                                       SUBSCRIPTION_PATH, 
                                                       executor=executor,

    sleep_cycles = 0

issue_title: gcloud==0.18.3 and google-cloud-datastore==1.2.0 run into dependency conflicts
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm working on a large project with a lot of history that's using some Google Cloud services. We use [pip-compile](https://github.com/jazzband/pip-tools) for generating our requirements file. I installed google-cloud-datastore==1.2.0, which worked fine, but after adding it in my requirements file and running `pip-compile`, I'm running in a quagmire of dependency conflicts. I've looked at #3579, but that didn't enlighten me much.

This is the error that `pip-compile` generates:

```
$ pip-compile --verbose --output-file requirements.txt requirements.in | grep ' requires .* google-gax'
  gcloud==0.18.3            requires gax-google-logging-v2<0.9dev,>=0.8.0, gax-google-pubsub-v1<0.9dev,>=0.8.0, google-gax<0.13dev,>=0.12.3, googleapis-common-protos, grpc-google-logging-v2<0.9dev,>=0.8.0, grpc-google-pubsub-v1<0.9dev,>=0.8.0, grpcio>=1.0rc1, httplib2>=0.9.1, oauth2client>=2.0.1, protobuf!=3.0.0.b2.post1,>=3.0.0b2, six
  google-cloud-datastore==1.2.0 requires gapic-google-cloud-datastore-v1<0.16dev,>=0.15.0, google-cloud-core<0.27dev,>=0.26.0, google-gax<0.16dev,>=0.15.7
Could not find a version that matches google-gax<0.13dev,<0.16dev,>=0.12.3,>=0.15.7
```

As you can see, the problem is trying to determine the correct version of google-gax, which gcloud requires to be <0.13dev,>=0.12.3, while google-cloud-datastore requires to be <0.16dev,>=0.15.7. Obviously, this results in an error.

I'm not sure what the steps forward are here. I'm surprised that pip allowed installing and using google-cloud-datastore at all. I'm assuming that in itself broke the usage of gcloud, but that is used in a lot of different places in the code that I can't test now to verify that it broke.

issue_title: PEX can't format an exception
issue_body:
If there are non-string values ```self._distributions``` [this](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/master/pex/pex_builder.py#L214) line does not work:
```
direvius@debuild-trusty:~/robe/yandex-tank$ pex -f wheelhouse/ yandextank -c yandex.tank -o yandex-tank.pex
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/pex", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pex/bin/pex.py", line 509, in main
    pex_builder = build_pex(reqs, options, resolver_options_builder)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pex/bin/pex.py", line 486, in build_pex
    pex_builder.set_script(options.script)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pex/pex_builder.py", line 214, in set_script
    script, ', '.join(self._distributions)))
TypeError: sequence item 0: expected string, DistInfoDistribution found
```
The solution is to convert them into strings before joining:
```
', '.join(str(d) for d in self._distributions)
```

issue_title: add a `-R` flag to pex(1) to support the ingestion of a structured dir of resources into an output pex
issue_body:
currently, `pex(1)` only operates against 3rdparty deps for composition of an output pex. in pants, we use pex as a library to create pex files that contain 3rdparty deps, sources and resources.

in order to help unblock utilization of `pex(1)` as a CLI tool in the v2 engine python pipeline porting, we'd like to add a flag to `pex(1)` to permit ingestion of a directory of resources into a pex. this will differ from sources ingestion because resources are never processed (e.g. byte compiled).

success criteria: can craft a pex via `pex(1)` that contains at least one resource file, which can be loaded with `pkg_resources` from the pex's repl.

issue_body:
We need to nail down a formalized policy for deprecations (options, modules, functions, etc) going into and after 1.0.0 that developers can follow that will improve the user experience around deprecations.

This should be documented in the developers guide as well as in the release process docs.

issue_title: Add a final timestamp to the pants console output
issue_body:
The last timestamp we see when running pants is the time that the last task started:

```
 ./pants compile examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/simple
INFO] Detected git repository at /Users/zundel/Src/pants on branch master

11:14:20 00:00 [main]
               (To run a reporting server: ./pants server)
11:14:20 00:00   [bootstrap]
11:14:21 00:01   [setup]
...
11:14:24 00:04       [checkstyle]
11:14:25 00:05     [scalastyle]
11:14:25 00:05     [cpp-compile]
               Waiting for background workers to finish.
               SUCCESS
```

In the example above, `11:14:25` is the time that `[cpp-compile] `was started and `00:05` is the elapsed time not including the time it took to run `[cpp-compile]`.

This is normally not a big deal, but when running our new ruby rspec task, we noticed that it didn't include the time that our tests took because it the last task in the output.

One thing we could do is to add a timestamp on the SUCCESS / FAILURE message.

Another option is to just add an additional placeholder in the output to show the last timestamp and total elapsed time:

```
11:14:25 00:05     [cpp-compile]
11:14:26 00:06 Completed
               Waiting for background workers to finish.
               SUCCESS
```

issue_title: Add a flag to enable use of `LegacyBuildGraph` without pantsd.
issue_body:
currently, to use the new engine-backed `LegacyBuildGraph` the daemon must be enabled and running. the scope of this task would be to add a flag (e.g. `--enable-new-graph`) which would cause pants to construct and use the `LegacyBuildGraph` for a non-daemon run.

this will enable more reliable integration testing against the new `BuildGraph` facade - sans any actual resident caching that the daemon provides.

issue_title: Enable implicit_sources by default
issue_body:
The `target-arguments.implicit_sources` is extremely helpful in encouraging good target hygiene. We should enable it by default, after a period in which we warn if it is disabled.

To avoid being too noisy with the warning, it should be possible to conditionally warn only if implicit sources is False, and implicit sources _would_ be applied for a target... ie: warn in locations where we would apply the option, despite it being disabled. Then, after a release cycle or two, we can invert the default for the option and deprecate it.

issue_title: Fail more obviously if someone passes in a bad value for `--jvm-distributions-paths`
issue_body:
I just spent a while trying to figure out why pants was "ignoring" my setting for `--jvm-distributions-paths`. It turned out that I'd missed a word in the documentation that said that the values needed to be _lists_. Unfortunately, nothing visibly broke in the code telling me I had screwed up, because strings are iterable, so the code happily proceeded to pass my single string into later parts of the code, which then searched for java in every individual character of my path.

It seems like the ideal place would be to assert that `paths` is a list in [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/java/distribution/distribution.py#L609). TBC, the code _will_ break if you pass in multiple matching OSes, because the normalization process will attempt to call `.extend` on a string in that case which fails loudly. But if you have a single OS (I was passing in `--jvm-distributions-paths="{'darwin': '/path/to/my/jvm'}"`) it'll happily go search for a viable java in `"/"`, `"p"`, `"a"`, and so on.

issue_title: user-data: flannel configuration setup fails on any node but the first
issue_body:
This line in `user-data` is responsible for setting up flannel through etcd: https://github.com/deis/deis/commit/445dbaecfadb1e2690734413cc43f30abcb13b21#diff-be438a7ce6a159afe476e6cb80c59eddR53

All nodes trying to `mk` this etcd key = first node succeeding, others failing. Eventually flanneld fails to start and that in turn makes docker fail to start.

I have already fixed this for my production cluster by changing it like this: (adding the error-supressing `-`)
```
ExecStartPre=-/usr/bin/etcdctl mk /coreos.com/network/config '{"Network": "10.244.0.0/16", "SubnetLen": 24, "SubnetMin": "10.244.0.0", "Backend": {"Type": "vxlan"}}'
```
`etcdctl set` would also work, but would overwrite any customization the user might apply.

PR coming later, this issue reminds me to do it.

issue_title: Allow all methods accepting `num_results` to take `num_results=None` to mean "all results"
issue_body:
This came up during work on #93 -- we discussed the fact that there's no obvious way to get "all of the results" when you don't know how many there are.

The subtle distinction between "all results" and "no limit" is that some APIs have a service-side limit on the total number of results returned.

So, when a `PaginatedResource` sees `num_results=None`, it should
1. Check if there is a `max_total_results` and set `num_results` to that if found
2. Skip `num_results`-related checking if `num_results is None`

issue_title: STUB: Add protection against eclipse attacks in Trinity.
issue_body:
See: http://www.cs.bu.edu/~goldbe/projects/eclipseEth

### What is wrong?

We should build in some basic protections against eclipse attacks.

### How can it be fixed

- Require that a certain percentage of the peer connections are initiated as outgoing connection requests.
    - Configuration parameter that sets required number of connections that originate from a dial-out connection
    - Configuration parameter that sets the ratio of allowed dial-in to dial-out connections.
- Ensure that the IP addresses for peer connections are not all to the same IP address.
    - Configuration parameter that sets the maximum number of connections to a single IP address that are allowed, default to something like 3.

issue_title: Python 2/3 cleanup
issue_body:
The Six library (at `delphin.lib.six`) is only used for a single thing (`raise from` in `delphin.tdl`), so it might be overkill to include it. It should perhaps be removed unless other compatibility measures are expected. Alternatively, the other places compatibility measures are used (look for ImportError or NameError in `delphin.interfaces.rest`, `delphin.interfaces.base`, and `delphin.mrs.components`) could use Six.

If it is removed, look for other places where things should be more compatible (e.g. `isinstance(x, str)`) and fix them.

issue_title: Anaconda installs into my system root on OS X
issue_body:
I went through the Anaconda installer and I think because the user interface is unclear it has installed itself into my system root. This is non-standard behaviour.

When installers on OS X ask for a destination, picking your main Hard Drive is considered to be the choice of disk where to install something, not the literal path intended.

After installation `which python` returns `//anaconda/bin/python` which is also an incorrect path for OS X.

issue_title: Modelsim - mem load command
issue_body:
I'm trying to use the "mem load command" from Modelsim, like the following example:

ui = VUnit.from_argv()
ui.set_sim_option('modelsim.vsim_flags',['-do ../../report_top.do'])

The contents of the report_top.do file are:
mem load -i ../../hist.mem -startaddress 0 -endaddress 3145727 /m60_report_top_tb/ddr_ctrl_model_u/mig_bhv_model_u/ram_s;

But the Modelsim log this warning:

Warning: Ignoring nested -do argument: "do ../../report_top.do"

I would like to know if is it possible to load memory contents?

issue_body:
I tried to get Questa 10.5 running and tried both the 32 Bit and the 64 Bit Version. As it turns out Mentor changed the encoding of modelsim.ini to UTF-8 and that yields following error when using it with VUnit:

> Traceback (most recent call last):

issue_title: timestamp= parameter seems to have no effect
issue_body:
Regardless of the time I put in the timestamp= parameter or @timestamp field of the payload, when indexing a message, the timestamp that gets placed on that message always seems to be the timestamp of when the data was indexed, as opposed to the date of the value in the timestamp= field or @timestamp within the payload! 

This breaks queuing logic that may be indexing messages that built up during an outage for example. When the outage is restored and the queue is emptied, all messages from within the queue get indexed on top of each other in terms of the timestamp due to this bug.

issue_title: Progress REST calls should only be made when --verbose is specified
issue_body:
Currently, when running any template-based commands (`vm create`), regardless of whether the user specifies --verbose, the Azure monitor REST calls are made to supply the progress information.  These calls should only be made when --verbose (or --debug) are explicitly specified because:

1. Unnecessary use of the customer's API request quota when it wasn't explicitly requested
2. Scripts have no need for this information (see 1)
3. There is no way to turn it off
4. It causes VCR test recordings to balloon (this is actually how I initially discovered this issue)

---

issue_title: `pip install azure-cli==0.1.1b2` broken! (no module named _logging)
issue_body:
I have a Docker container where I pin to a specific version of the CLI:

```
ENV AZURE_CLI_VERSION 0.1.1b2
...
RUN pip install azure-cli==${AZURE_CLI_VERSION}
```

Obviously the first thing we do is run `az login ...`...

Today someone reported it didn't work. Turns out, works on my machine because the layers were cached, presumably from before `0.1.1b3` was released. So, I cleared all my cached docker layers and images and executed it again... and sure enough mine doesn't work now either.

```
$ az login

root@7b04240ef178:/gopath/src/github.com/Azure/acs-engine# az login
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/runpy.py", line 174, in _run_module_as_main
    "__main__", fname, loader, pkg_name)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/runpy.py", line 72, in _run_code
    exec code in run_globals
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/cli/__main__.py", line 9, in <module>
    import azure.cli.main
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/cli/main.py", line 10, in <module>
    import azure.cli.core._logging as _logging
ImportError: No module named _logging
```

`0.1.1b3` works, but I really don't want this to break in another 2 weeks

issue_title: [Generic Update] @file does not work in --set
issue_body:
The syntax for the generic update set option is:
`--set property=value`

If you try to use something like:
`--set property=@file` to load a JSON file as the value, the @ sign expansion will not occur.

issue_title: memory leak in getJointStates
issue_body:
Apparently there is a memory leak in the **p.getJointStates** method which doesn't occur for the **p.getJointState** method. Especially when using pybullet for reinforcement learning this can cause problems. 

Here is a little test script to reproduce the error: 
[bullet_mem_leak.tar.gz](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/files/1967018/bullet_mem_leak.tar.gz)

In line 39 the getJointStates method is called, which increases the memory usage gradually. Every 1000 iterations the memory information is printed. If in contrast getJointState is used, there is no memory leak. Additionally by uncommenting line 49 one can see the cause of the leak, a list is created every call of the method which isn't freed afterwards anymore.

issue_title: Slow Rendering in pybullet
issue_body:
For RL I would like to have pybullet render as fast as possible. How would I best achieve this?
I tested the three connection modes with ER_BULLET_HARDWARE_OPENGL and got the following fps:

DIRECT +shadow 29 ( defaults to ER_TINY_RENDERER?)
DIRECT -shadow 47 (defaults to ER_TINY_RENDERER?)
SHARED_MEMORY +shadow  29 ( using App_PhysicsServer_SharedMemory)
SHARED_MEMORY -shadow   47 ( using App_PhysicsServer_SharedMemory)
(to test FPS times I used: https://gist.github.com/BlGene/9010fb3671dac3cc566ee659fcda155a)

The GUI or SHARED_MEMORY + App_ExampleBrowser connections run very slowly for me <10fps. This seems to work better others, a youtube video (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=__ilQzkPDNI)  mentions the ExampleBrowser running at 1000fps. 

The difference may be due to pybullet is selecting the wrong GPU on my machine (NVS 310 vs  GeForce GTX 1070).   Is there a way to control which GPU is used by pybullet?

Training for RL with the GUI connection would require the option not to open the ExampleBrowser window. I expected COV_ENABLE_GUI to do this. Would this be sufficient to allow running in headless mode?

The following line the documentation is confusing for me:
    "Note that DIRECT mode has no OpenGL, so it requires ER_TINY_RENDERER."
From this I understood that App_PhysicsServer_SharedMemory uses OpenGL, although its never explicitly stated. This does however not appear to be the cause as shown by fps performance and GPU memory consumption.

To get fast OpenGL based rendering another option would be to use the recently introduced dynamically loaded render plugins. One approach would be to adapt the roboschool rendering as a plugin. But before I attempt this I would like to ask if it is this a reasonable approach and if this code has already been written by somebody else.

Thanks for any help in advance.

issue_title: Client max size ignored on cloning web request object
issue_body:
## Long story short

`_client_max_size` private attr not specified on cloning `web_request.BaseRequest` object.

## Expected behaviour

When `web.Application` using custom `client_max_size` and in same time some middleware that cloned request, like [https_middleware](http://aiohttp-middlewares.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#aiohttp-https), there is **still able** to upload any file larger then 1Mb as default `Request._client_max_size` will be overwritten with value from `Application`.

## Actual behaviour

When `web.Application` using custom `client_max_size` and in same time some middleware that cloned request, like [https_middleware](http://aiohttp-middlewares.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#aiohttp-https), there is **unable** to upload any file larger then 1Mb as default `Request._client_max_size` not overwritten with value from `Application` after `request.clone` call.

## Steps to reproduce

- Enable [https_middleware](http://aiohttp-middlewares.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#aiohttp-https) or any other middleware which clone `request` before passing to inner handler
- Run aiohttp under nginx with HTTPS support
- Upload any file larger then 1Mb
- Get `ValueError` on `await request.post()` or `HTTPRequestEntityTooLarge` on `await request.read()`

## Your environment

- `Python==3.6.3`
- `aiohttp==2.3.1`

issue_title: Slow _filter_existing_notebooks impacts each submission
issue_body:
On a deployment with ~600 students, _filter_existing_notebooks takes about 30s. This hits us when manual grading. A single submission is loaded (/formgrader/submissions/:submission_id) and that invokes api.get_notebook_submission_indices which calls the filter which walks the filesystem and filters out non-existing files. So when the grader presses Next, the next submission is loaded which causes another 30s filesystem walk.

### Operating system

### `nbgrader --version`
0.6.0.dev
### `jupyterhub --version` (if used with JupyterHub)
0.8.1
### `jupyter notebook --version` 
5.4.0
### Expected behavior
Indexes are cached or are built asynchronously?
### Actual behavior
Indexes are gathered for each submission load.
### Steps to reproduce the behavior
Manual grading in big course, visit assignment. Then visit first submission.

issue_title: How to archive real time communication between Python and JS ?
issue_body:
My goal is to run JS canvas-game within a notebook and get continues data stream between itself and Python. First i have tried to execute notebook kernel from JS, 60 times per second, with an animation frame request hook. But then it hit me, this process is working on a single kernel. Here is my initial attempt:
```js
%%javascript
window.onEnterFrame = e => IPython.notebook.kernel.execute(
`sharedVariable = "${ window.getGameVar() }"  `
);
```
So that resulted in non changing sharedVariable value during the cell execution
```python
import time
result_1 = sharedVariable
time.sleep( 1 )
result_2 = sharedVariable
print( result_1 == result_2 ) # output is always True
```
After which i have decided to try and use the COMM, so I made a simple one that will replay with a string value after 500ms of delay.
```js
%%javascript
Jupyter.notebook.kernel.comm_manager.register_target('my_comm_id', (comm, msg)=> {
    comm.on_msg(_=> { // on msg received 
        setTimeout( _=> { // wait 500ms
            comm.send(  window.getGameVar()  ); // output data
        }, 500 );
    });
});
```
And a matching Python COMM:
```python
my_comm = Comm( target_name='my_comm_id' )
@my_comm.on_msg
def _recv(msg):
    global sharedVariable
    sharedVariable = msg['content']['data']
```
But that didn't help either, as apparently the messaged were getting received only after the cell had completed it's execution
```python
my_comm.send( "" )
time.sleep( 1 )
result_1 = sharedVariable
my_comm.send( "" )
time.sleep( 1 )
result_2 = sharedVariable
print( result_1 == result_2 ) # output is always True
``` 
Also i have tried creating a separate async function 
```python
import asyncio

async def getData2(future):
    global my_comm, sharedVariable
    my_comm.send( "" )
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    future.set_result( sharedVariable )

def getData():
    asyncio.set_event_loop(asyncio.new_event_loop()) # set new global event loop
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    future = asyncio.Future()
    asyncio.ensure_future( getData2(future) )
    loop.run_until_complete(future)
    result = future.result()
    loop.close()
    return result
```
But the problem didn't go away
```python
result_1 = getData()
result_2 = getData()
print( result_1 == result_2 ) # output is always True
```

I had a brief overview of `ipywidgets` and was able to get one way communication to work like so:
```
from ipywidgets import FloatSlider
from IPython.display import display
slider = FloatSlider()
display(slider)
for i in range( 11 ):
    slider.value = i * 10    
    time.sleep( 1 )
```
But the widgets won't work if you change the slider by dragging it
```python
for i in range( 10 ):
    print( slider.value )    
    time.sleep( 1 ) # Will always output slider initial output
```

issue_title: DataError raised when validate=False
issue_body:
Let's say we have the following model;

``` python
class Foo(Model):
    bar = BooleanType()
```

And then we create an instance of it;

``` python
a = Foo(dict(bar='a'), validate=False)
```

This raises `DataError` (obviously because `bar` is not a bool) however I'd rather that DataError is not raised until I specifically call `a.validate()`.

Are there any workarounds for this or is this something that is planned to be rectified (perhaps as part of #369)?

The reason why this is important to me is because I want all errors to be reported to the user, not just some of them, only for them to resubmit their request to find out there are more errors. For example;

``` python
class Foo(Model):
    bar = BooleanType()
    eggs = StringType(choices=['yes', 'no', 'maybe'])
```
1. User submits a form with `bar='a'` and `eggs='adsf'`
2. User is notified of an error about `bar` not being valid.
3. User fixes `bar` and resubmits form
4. User is notified for more errors for `eggs`

From the users point of view that is extremely frustrating and from a programmers point of view even more frustrating that you can't do this!

Obviously this is a pretty trivial example and it's unlikely given the datatypes you would give the user a free form text field, however you get my point.

----> 1 cfg = giraffez.config.Config(mode='w')

/opt/anaconda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/giraffez/config.py in __init__(self, conf, mode, key_file)
    127         if key_file is None:
    128             self._key_file = home_file(".giraffepg")
--> 129         self._key_file = _get_key_file(key_file)

This is either a bug in the code or a bug in the docs, and I'd advocate for it being a bug in the code. issue_title: ToilJenkinsSlave: SLURM setup is sensitive to instance type
issue_body:
[Changing](https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/toil-scripts/pull/251#issuecomment-214912601) the instance type of a toil-jenkins-slave to a type with less memory causes Toil's SLURM tests to hang.

I believe this is due to the fact that the available memory is evaluated at `cgcloud create` time and the baked into the config file. The memory should be evaluated every time the machine starts up.

issue_title: Requesting support for Linux-ARM w/ 16 FPU registers
issue_body:
Hi,

Our Business Units widely use ARM based CPU to develop hardware solutions for automation and energy management.

We appreciate Microsoft’s decision to support native .Net Core deployment for linux-arm target. We have positive results of self-contained deployments for Cortex-A9 based devices. It’ll really speed up development-to-deployment-to-market process.

During one of our POC projects, we’ve identified that some of chips (e.q. RZ/N1D), extensively used by our BU, aren’t compatible with current linux-arm runtime and generate “Illegal Instruction” exception.

Further investigation revealed very simple but critical incompatibility: this chip’s FPU includes only 16 double-precision registers. Seems like simple change of cross-compiler option from vfpv3 to vfpv3-d16 [or vfpv4-d16] to generate runtime libraries should solve this problem. 

An alternative solution would be to support linux-armel target, i.e. do not use Floating Point Unit at all, though the previously outlined solution would be preferable.

Would you please consider building and including runtime specific packages for linux-arm-VFPv4-d16 and/or linux-armel devices.

issue_title: Some data lost using Confluent-kafka-python's producer send message  to Kafka
issue_body:
Descriptions
==========

> We develop a logparsing agent to parsing mysql's binary log, then translate the event to json message ,  using Confluent-kafka-python to send the message to kafka.  If i do flush after every message send, no data lose,  if i send it in async mode , some data lose Random. 

My code like this : 
============
## Init producer 
In the parsing thread do init the producer and do the message sending , 
the parsing thread has a method to do producer flush. this method can be called 
by another thread called checkpoint periodicly. 

```
  ##  Init 
    def _init_producer(self):
        cnf = {
            'bootstrap.servers': gv.kafka_hosts.strip(),
            'compression.type': 'gzip',
            'retries': 10,
            'acks': 2,
            'max.in.flight.requests.per.connection': 1,
            'queue.buffering.max.messages': 1000000,
            'queue.buffering.max.kbytes': 536870912,
            'fetch.wait.max.ms': 50
        }
        try:
            self.producer = Producer(cnf)
        except Exception, e:
            print e
            logger.error('[%s]: THDdumper initproducer %s ' %
                         (self.name.ljust(10), traceback.format_exc()))
```
## The message Sender and checkpoint method  
```
    def _send_event(self, msg):
        self.counter = self.counter + 1
        try:
            tab = msg['table']
            schema = msg['schema']
            key = schema + '.' + tab
            if key in gv.topics_map.keys():
                self.logger.info("[%s]: _send_event(msg) : %s " %
                                 (self.name.ljust(10), json.dumps(msg)))
                topics = gv.topics_map[key]
                # dump msg
                msg = json.dumps(msg)
                for top in topics:
                    self.producer.produce(
                        top, msg,
                        callback=delivery_callback
                    )
                    self.producer.poll(0)
        except Exception, e:
            logger.error('[%s]: _send_event() exception %s ' %
                         (self.name.ljust(10), traceback.format_exc()))
            logger.error('[%s]: _send_event() exception %s ' %
                         (self.name.ljust(10), str(e)))
            raise e

    ## call by checkpointer thread 
    def check_point(self):
        self.logger.info("[%s]: check_point() " % (self.name.ljust(10)))
        self.producer.flush(60) 
```
 In checkpointer thread has a method like this:
```
    def _flush_point(self):
        # triger the checkpoint in dumper thread
        if gv.checkpointer.isAlive():
            gv.workers['dumper'].check_point()
```

Checklist
=========
the lib's version below:
  - confluent-kafka==0.11.4

issue_title: Salt correction params ignored in hairpin,etc calcs
issue_body:
Hi Primer3-py,

According to the following post, the methods: calcHeterodimer, calcHairpin, etc. should be able to use the tm_method and salt_correction_method, is that something you could make primer3-py support as well?
https://sourceforge.net/p/primer3/feature-requests/25/

Here is a code example showing the issue:

    >>> primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis(salt_correction_method=0).calcHeterodimer('TGAGGCACCTATCTCAGCGA','TCGCTGAGATAGGTGCCTCA').tm
    62.99575324661981
    >>> primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis(salt_correction_method=1).calcHeterodimer('TGAGGCACCTATCTCAGCGA','TCGCTGAGATAGGTGCCTCA').tm
    62.99575324661981
    >>> primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis(salt_correction_method=0).calcTm('TGAGGCACCTATCTCAGCGA')
    59.81988509331603
    >>> primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis(salt_correction_method=1).calcTm('TGAGGCACCTATCTCAGCGA')
    63.07165934075988

As you can see, for the hetero dimer calculations, the salt_correction_method has no influence.

Best, Martin

issue_title: Connection issues with own metakernel
issue_body:
Hi,

I have recently ported my own Python-based Jupyter kernel to derive from metakernel instead of ipykernel. 
Since then, I had the following issues:

- I get "forbidden" errors such as 
```
[W 14:42:21.580 NotebookApp] Forbidden
[W 14:42:21.580 NotebookApp] 403 GET /api/config/common?_=1520862141398 (127.0.0.1) 1.43ms referer=http://localhost:8889/notebooks/ ...
```
which is possibly resolved by using the `--NotebookApp.token=''` flag

- The %connect_info magic gives this info:
```
{
  "stdin_port": UNKNOWN,
  "shell_port": UNKNOWN,
  "iopub_port": UNKNOWN,
  "hb_port": UNKNOWN,
  "ip": "UNKNOWN",
  "key": "UNKNOWN",
  "signature_scheme": "UNKNOWN",
  "transport": "UNKNOWN"
}
```
which should be more sensible, comparing to the metakernel echo example.

- when playing around with matplotlib, I got errors saying that there are no `comm` and interactive shell instances - but then I am not sure if there should be.

Any ideas on what is happening here? 
I think that I have adapted the necessary lines from the examples to my kernel. 
Do you have recommendations on how to debug these errors?

issue_title: Docs are outdated (currently 1.2)
issue_body:
The [latest docs](http://folium.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) encourage use of `.simple_marker`, which results in a deprecation notice. Similar code is on the Readme for the Github page. There doesn't seem to be any mention of `add_children` in the docs or the Readme. 

I've run into a couple other differences between the online docs and the code, which have presented a series of hurdles trying to use folium.

issue_title: Cannot detect Windows Arrow Keys
issue_body:
Hi there,

Having problems sending arrow key events in Command.exe on Windows 10. I am using a serial connection to a Micropython board ( https://vgkits.org/blog/2018/05/08/introductory-video-tutorial/ ) as a worked example.

Not sure if this is a different issue to https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/issues/207 as the modified version of miniterm indicated in that thread doesn't seem to resolve the issue.

On Mac and Linux, miniterm seems to be fine, launched as documented here...
https://vgkits.org/blog/connecting-with-miniterm/
...and the arrow keys work fine on Mac and Linux too.

However, the Windows invocation documented there creates a console session in which arrow keys and backspace keys are junked.

The invocation I tried most recently on Windows (note this was an attempt to use the 'patched miniterm-MPY') was...

```bash
python miniterm_mpy.py --raw --eol CRLF COM3 --encoding ascii 115200
```

This leads to a terminal in which pressing the up key produces first...

```
?H?
```

...and then if you press again it seems to send an up arrow key and shows...

```
 clearPixel()
```

...then if you press again it shows 

```
 clearPixel()[12DsetPixel(0, red)
```
...and finally...
```
 clearPixel()[12DsetPixel(0, red)[16D[K4+4
```

What it should show is single, clear lines drawn from the history one by one as you press up and down like...

```
clearPixel()
setPixel(0, red)
4+4
```

I'd really like to know that I don't have to guide users to run everything inside the Ubuntu subsystem on Windows 10 just to get a terminal to work, but it sounds like that might be true.

issue_title: i = seru.read()
Investigate handling of image compression in object detector
issue_body:
The current object detector implementation converts all the images into a raw format up front, to avoid paying the cost of decompression for each training epoch. However, with better pipelining and data preparation, the biggest gaps in GPU utilization currently arise from shuffling the SFrame after each epoch.

We should investigate keeping the images compressed in the SFrame and decoding them on the fly when preparing inputs for the GPU (when GPU training is available). If this cost can be entirely or mostly hidden, then the shuffle operations should become much faster.

issue_title: build and serve docs using ghpages in addition to rtfd.org
issue_body:
Priority: Very very low

Since there seem to be occasional gliches and errors serving docs at [rtfd.org](rtfd.org) maybe the ghpages at pvlib.github.io/pvlib-python should be activated. I know it's an additional PITA, but  maybe there are also upsides like a chance to test rendered docs, an alternate site in case rtfd goes down? [Maybe there is a way to automate build and deploy using travis?](https://gist.github.com/domenic/ec8b0fc8ab45f39403dd). [It seems like there are a few examples?](https://gist.github.com/domenic/ec8b0fc8ab45f39403dd) [Here's a blog post](http://www.steveklabnik.com/automatically_update_github_pages_with_travis_example/) and [an article in medium](https://medium.com/@nthgergo/publishing-gh-pages-with-travis-ci-53a8270e87db#.b75zbk2g8).

Anyway, just an idea, based on the [issue with forecasting docs described in this SO post](http://stackoverflow.com/a/41686297/1020470).

issue_title: Misuse of Python language feature
issue_body:
In looking at the code to do my own implementation, I noticed the misuse of the double underscore (__) in the code base.  The code here: https://github.com/oasis-open/cti-pattern-matcher/blob/master/stix2matcher/matcher.py#L2125 accesses self.__parse_tree.  This "should" not work, as the double underscore is intended to prevent two different classes from stepping on each other: https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/classes.html#private-variables-and-class-local-references  This happens to work because both classes are named Pattern, even though it should not, as they are unrelated classes.  The issue is at the assignment: https://github.com/oasis-open/cti-pattern-validator/blob/master/stix2patterns/pattern.py#L34

There should probably be a method added to the stix2patterns.pattern.Pattern class that accepts and object, and calls antlr4.ParseTreeWalker.DEFAULT.walk w/ the object and __parse_tree. 
 __parse_tree should be renamed to _parse_tree as that is a more common method of denoting non-public attributes and methods.

issue_title: Hook an address (or entire memory range) with a custom SimEngine
issue_body:
Following @zardus comment on #527 

> Also, an idea for a future improvement that just popped up: it might be cool to be able to hook an address (or entire memory range) with a custom SimEngine, so that it gets called for any address there. That could provide a nice interface that's more general than hooking an address with a SimProcedure, while being less deeply invasive than a SimInspect hook. We can also use this functionality to get rid of the looping-through-each-engine logic here.

I think it's a great idea, as this will make the overall angr architecture less _tight-coupled_ (or more _modular_) and flexible. The first _pros_ that comes to my mind are:

- `SimProcedure` handling will be a less-special case than it is now (no more stop_points at `SimEngineVEX`, no more checking of whether the address is hooked in `SimEngineHook`, etc.)
- The hook/unhook logic will be separated from the `Project`, and moved to somewhere it truly belongs, for example, a `SimMetaEngine` class, which will provide a generic hook/unhook/process methods.

The proposed implementation here is to introduce a new class called `SimMetaEngine` (name suggestions are welcome!). The `SimMetaEngine` would behave just like the `SimEngine` base class, expect that the process method won't take any actions on the provided state, but, instead, it will select an appropriate set of engines, which are hooked (or marked for execution) to the current address range, and iteratively try to make them process the state. For this to be done, the internal _hook map_ should be maintained in some form of a ranged dictionary. The keys for the _hook map_ are the address ranges, and the values are the ordered sets of the engines, which should be iteratively tried in order to process the state. In this case, an address range could be hooked with one or several engines, thus providing a fallback solution, if the first engine didn't work.

That's it. Sorry for bad English, I hope you understood my idea correctly. Looking forward to hear what you think about it.

issue_title: Proposal for improving handling of broken (sub)jobs
issue_body:
Hi all,

There are some users again reporting that the monitoring isn't picking up DIRAC jobs with subjobs in the `submitting` state.

I can't think of any meaningful way that _any_ of the current backends can deal with monitoring a job in the `submitting` state. I could be wrong, but to me if the job is in this state in the XML it indicates that something failed.
If a (sub)job has been loaded from disk and is in this state then the (sub)job is, I suspect by definition in a bad state. To get here something has crashed/failed or ganga was force exited or killed during the job submission so some or all of the monitoring information has probably been lost.


Can we consider force failing a job automatically if it's loaded from disk in a `submittting` state?


These (sub)jobs seem to be confusing the monitoring as some people are still seeing ganga struggling to update jobs with a subjob in the `submitting` state.

I think a short PR could accomplish this from the XML layer but what are people's opinions on this?

Rob

issue_title: Retrieving last_modified information for "hidden" buckets
issue_body:
The `/permissions` endpoint allows inferring parent containers for resources the authenticated user has access to, which is great.

For example, when I get this entry:

```json
{
  "bucket_id": "blocklists-preview",
  "resource_name": "collection",
  "collection_id": "addons",
  "permissions": [
    "read",
    "read:attributes"
  ],
  "uri": "/buckets/blocklists-preview/collections/addons",
  "id": "addons"
}
```

I know I have read access to the `addons` collection in the `blocklists-preview` bucket, while the `blocklists-preview` itself is not listed if I query the `/buckets` endpoint. That's the only way I'm aware of to know about this information.

But we can't know about the `last_modified` of that bucket; no way to query the `buckets/blocklists-preview` endpoint of I'm not allowed to, neither it can be listed under `/buckets` for the same reason.

Do you know any other way to access this information?

Context: having orderable entries in the kinto-admin sidebar (https://github.com/Kinto/kinto-admin/issues/118).

issue_title: Delete permissions should be different from write permission same to give permissions.
issue_body:
As per discussion with @chrismbeckett on IRC:

In business application, having write permissions doesn't means nor that you can delete the data nor that you can change its permissions.

We should probably let people define another sharable model for their Kinto Instance.

Today we have:

 - ProtectedResource
 - SharableResource

We may want to let people define other ones more complex with their own inheritance tree and another set of permissions or at least let them define which verb are link to which permission.

Keep in mind that without a collection schema it is possible to remove a records by putting an empty record instead, with the collection schema it is not possible to remove something by putting an empty record for instance but it is still possible to override it with wrong data.

Maybe we should investigate this use case and see what are the options to handle that.

issue_title: Poor performance in Django 1.11 after template-based widget rendering
issue_body: As reported in Django's Trac ticket [#27774](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/27774), enabling the "Templates" panel on a page that does a lot of template-widget rendering ([#15667](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/15667)) results in unacceptably slow page load. issue_title: Wildcard upload requires confirmation
issue_body:
Conan will ask for confirmation (yes/no) if the upload reference is a pattern.
The packager should probably pass `--confirm` to `conan upload` since it's purpose is mostly for CI/automation. Without `--confirm` my CI job creates an infinite large log when it hangs at:

    Are you sure you want to upload '...'? (yes/no): ERROR:  is not a valid answer
    Are you sure you want to upload '...'? (yes/no): ERROR:  is not a valid answer
    ...

issue_title: Clean up THCI script for dataset configuration.
issue_body:
Currently for the purpose of being able to response MGMT_ACTIVE_GET.req after device just resets, we call `dataset commit active` along within each API that configures the network parameter (i.e. channel, panid, networkname, masterkey ....). That will cause flash writing operation each time. For now, we have persistent storage to store the dataset, we can enhance this part and remove the unnecessary configuration.

Another enhancement is to initialize all Active Dataset Tlvs when Thread Device becomes Leader.

issue_title: Ansible module variables
issue_body:
Hello all,
Thanks for this project in the first place. 

In the beginning I had problem with usage of the napalm_install_config module. 
Just tried using variables "commit_changes" and "replace_config" with parameters "true" and "false". 
I just get used to it with other unusable modules. 

Is there any particular reason why only values True/False and 1/0 are only possible.

Just get simple PoC in my repo with adding type bool for these variables.
https://github.com/ppieprzycki/napalm/blob/master/ansible/napalm_install_config


In the book that I'm currently studying "Ansible up and running"  i can find some explanation 

>Ansible is pretty flexible on how you represent truthy and falsey values in playbooks. Strictly speaking, >module arguments (like update_cache=yes) are treated differently from values elsewhere in playbooks >(like sudo: True). Values elsewhere are handled by the YAML parser and so use the YAML conventions >of truthiness, which are:
>YAML truthy
>true, True, TRUE, yes, Yes, YES, on, On, ON, y, Y YAML falsey
>false, False, FALSE, no, No, NO, off, Off, OFF, n, N
>
>Module arguments are passed as strings and use Ansible’s internal conventions, which are:
>
>module arg truthy
>yes, on, 1, true 
> module arg falsey
>no, off, 0, false
>
>I tend to follow the examples in the official Ansible documentation. 
>These typically use yes and no when passing arguments to modules 
>(since that’s consistent with the module documentation), and True and False elsewhere in playbooks.

Please share some thoughts about this 

Regards
Piotr Pieprzycki

issue_title: Exposing base member from derived, when base is not wrapped
issue_body:
If I have a base class `B` with a data member `x` (of type `float`, but that is irrelevant) and a derived class `D`. And if I now expose only `D` (because `B` for some other reason should not be exposed) as: `py::class_<D>(m, "D").def(py::init<>()).def_readonly("x", &D::x);` Accessing `x` from Python fails with:

```
E TypeError: (): incompatible function arguments. The following argument types are supported:
E 1. (self: B) -> float
E
E Invoked with: <pybind11_tests.D object at 0x108e2e538>

```

This can be fixed with an explicit cast as in `.def_readonly("x", (float D::*) &D::x);`, but that should not really be required (as pointed out my @jagerman on Gitter: Pybind doesn't know anything about the `D : B` relationship here, which is why it happens. It ought to be possible to make it work properly (and `static_assert` fail when the member isn't a superclass member)).

issue_title: allow for generic script workflow

Right now, creating scripts for scriptworker is a bit more involved than it should be.  Not only do the scripts have to perform the action, but they also have to verify the schema and scopes before proceeding.  It would be good if we could get scriptworker to do some of this.

For example, in scriptworker.yml we could allow for a task jsonschema file.  We could add a dict of scopes to script commandlines (this would need some way to specify getting info from the task definition).

If we make these changes, many scripts may be runnable in scriptworker without [as many?] changes.

issue_title: 'RaidenService' object has no attribute 'on_message'
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

`RaidenService.on_message` is assigned in the `__init__` method after the `AlarmTask` gets started (and callbacks are registered on it). This causes a race condition since network messages can arrive before the `on_message` method is available which then leads to `AttributeError: 'RaidenService' object has no attribute 'on_message'`

## Solution

Delay starting the alarm task until after the `on_message` attribute has been set.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Delay starting the alarm task until after the `on_message` attribute has been set.

issue_body:
## Problem Definition

In case the tx polling in `ContractDiscovery.register()` times out after `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT` (currently 180s) either due to the eth node not being available or congestion, Raiden lingers in an unusable state.

issue_title: Output `group_by` option
issue_body:
Currently we have input options  `group_by` and `paired_with`. But now I have a workflow that generates multiple output from a single input. I wonder how this can be implemented. For example, I have a step that takes 2 input files and produce 3 output files per run:

```
[1]
input: something, group_by = 2
output: something_else, ??group_by = 3??
task: concurrent = True
R:
 produce_output(${_input[0]}, ${_input[1]}, ${_output[0]}, ${_output[1]}, ${_output[2]})
```

How can this be implemented in the current SoS framework? 

BTW I find the `reference manual` of SoS wiki very helpful but there is no `search` feature. Do you think it is good to have that? Maybe I should open a separate ticket for it.

issue_title: Question: automatically zap input files?
issue_body:
[Also from my colleague] consider a workflow that looks like this (code will not work, just showing idea):

```
[1]
input: for_each = ['files_sra'], group_by = 1, concurrent = True
output: files_sra
download: 
  {url}/{_files_sra}

[2]
output: files_bam
run:
 sratools-dump ${_input} ${_output}
```

The problem is that the data size is huge and can barely fit into a disk. So ideally we need to immediately `zap` output of step 1 after step 2 is done. Is there a build-in mechanism for it?

issue_title: Services from `/arlas/explore/*` accepting a filter in the `GET` or `POST` should also accept a filter in the `HEADER`
issue_body:
Services from `/arlas/explore/*` accepting a filter in the `GET` or `POST` should also accept a filter in the `HEADER`.
The filter is specified in the JSON format and a `AND` operator is applied between the filter provided in the GET/POST and the filter provided in the `HEADER`.

Note: In some cases, the filter is currently appended as `OR`, if for example two `equal` operators are applied on a same field. This behavior is not acceptable for the present case : a real  `AND` operator must be applied.

issue_title: Deferred status transition with Lpar.activate() and deactivate()
issue_labels: [{'id': 429586299, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/area:%20code', 'name': 'area: code', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 429588767, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/resolution:%20fixed', 'name': 'resolution: fixed', 'color': 'cccccc', 'default': False}, {'id': 429587821, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/type:%20feature', 'name': 'type: feature', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
When the `Lpar.activate()` and `deactivate()` methods complete (e.g. by returning after being called with `wait_for_completion=True`), the LPAR state may not have reached the desired state, and a subsequent status retrieval still reports the old state.

This does not happen always, and the exact circumstances are not fully understood at this point.

As a result, users of the zhmcclient have to code around this and loop on status retrieval until the desired state is reached.

The zhmcclient could take care of that by performing the stats polling inside of these methods for the `wait_for_completion=True` case, making life easier for the user. In the `wait_for_completion=False` case, adressing this is more complex, but not impossible, given that we have PR #182: The async method could return a new subclass of `Job` that implements its check and wait methods such that completion includes polling for the desired status.

Discussion points:
* Should we do this at all?
* Should a timeout be added? (see Issue #6)

issue_title: Segfault in plasma manager when getting many objects on multiple machines.
issue_body:
The plasma manager can be made to segfault as follows.

1. Start a large number of nodes (it may actually be possible to reproduce this on one or two nodes). However, on 100 m4.xlarge instances, this happens consistently.

2. Start Ray on all of the machines. You may want to start the Ray processes on the head node separately so that you can start the plasma manager in GDB. To do this, follow the instructions in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/108#issuecomment-308572084.

3. Run the following workload (after initializing Ray).

    ```python
    @ray.remote
    def f():
        return 1

    %time l = ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(1000000)])

issue_title: Testing for CacheManger.get_graph_by_filter
[Core] Introduction of Data Manager
issue_body:
With the PRs #879 and #880 I've created this concept of a data manager, something that allows you to change physical locations of where you want data stored (which is something we haven't had before). It fixes two big issues, first your data no longer has to be in the same folder as the bot files, and second it now gets rid of the need to set the CWD to the bot's folder.

### Why is this important?

If (when) we get pip installation working, we cannot store data inside the folder where the bot is installed. These PRs eliminate that problem but they do introduce new things that need to be discussed and decided.

"Config" is a high level, abstracted way to interact with cog data. It is highly dependent on knowing where to look and save data *during* the config object creation. This means that in order for config to work we must know where (and how) data is being saved.

### Possible solutions

We know we're probably going to want two scripts, one to generate the information that config needs and one to actually run the bot, so the question is what will the first script generate.

1. The script could generate a file named `config.json` in the CWD that contains the information necessary to get the bot up and running.

2. To more nicely support multiple bots, the script could ask for an instance name and save multiple sets of startup information in a single `config.json` which would be placed somewhere like `~/.config` or `%APPDATA`

### Thoughts?

issue_title: File staging of unknown schemes fails silently
issue_body:
When an unknown URL scheme is used for a URL, no staging happens, and the input filename on execution side resolves to the empty string.
A better user experience would be to give a more explicit error.

An example piece of code is pasted here. This code runs successfully despite the file URL not existing, 
and despite an explicit check with `-f` for file existence in the bash code.

```
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import parsl
from parsl import File
from parsl import DataFlowKernel
from parsl import DataFlowKernelLoader
from parsl.config import *
from parsl.app.app import bash_app

parsl.set_stream_logger()

c = Config(executors=[ThreadPoolExecutor()])

dfk = DataFlowKernel(config = c)

# workaround, issue #381
DataFlowKernelLoader._dfk = dfk

@bash_app(data_flow_kernel = dfk)
def foo(inputs=[]):
  return ("ls ; echo X{}X ; [ -f {} ] ".format(inputs[0], inputs[0]))


myfile = File("myproto://stuff.txt")

future = foo(inputs=[myfile])

print("RESULT: {}".format(future.result()))
```

issue_title: lark does not tokenize properly
issue_body:
I have the following grammar:

```python
parser = Lark(r'''
input                : word+
word                 : unquoted | lquoted | squoted | dquoted

lquoted               : /\$'[^']*'/
squoted               : /'[^']*'/
dquoted               : /"(?:[^"]+|\\.)*"/
unquoted              : /(?:[^\s()&;|<>]+|\\.)+/

%import common.WS
%ignore WS
''', start='input', parser='lalr')
```

When I try to parse a string like `$'abcd'`, this is what I get:

```
> $'abcd'
input
  word
    unquoted	$'abcd'
```

However, on the basis of the rules, shouldn't the result yield a `lquoted` instead of a `unquoted`?
